techniques.name: Figure-4 Deadfall Trap
techniques.subtitle: Heavy rock triggered by bait stick

steps.instruction: Find a flat heavy rock (5-20 lbs depending on target).
steps.helpDetail: Rock must be heavy enough to kill the animal instantly. A suffering animal will escape. Test weight by imagining it dropped on prey.

steps.instruction: Carve 3 sticks: vertical post, diagonal bait stick, horizontal trigger.
steps.helpDetail: Each stick has notches that interlock. The figure-4 shape is critical — it converts light trigger force into total collapse.

steps.instruction: Assemble under the rock: vertical stick supports horizontal trigger, diagonal holds bait.
steps.helpDetail: The bait stick angles toward the ground under the rock. When the animal pulls the bait, it releases the trigger, dropping the rock.

steps.instruction: Place bait on the end of the diagonal stick.
steps.helpDetail: Use peanut butter, berries, seeds, or meat scraps. Set on game trails. Check twice daily — trapped animals attract predators.

techniques.name: Simple Wire Snare
techniques.subtitle: Noose trap for small game

steps.instruction: Make a noose loop from wire, paracord, or strong cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Wire snares are most effective — animals can't chew through. The loop should be fist-sized for rabbits, head-sized for larger game.

steps.instruction: Secure the free end to a stake, tree, or spring pole.
steps.helpDetail: A spring pole (bent sapling) lifts the catch off the ground, preventing escape and keeping it from other predators.

steps.instruction: Set at head height on an active game trail.
steps.helpDetail: Position the bottom of the loop 2-3 inches off the ground for rabbits. Look for droppings, tracks, and worn paths.

steps.instruction: Set 10-12 snares to increase your odds.
steps.helpDetail: One snare has maybe a 10% chance. Twelve snares give you a reasonable chance of catching something. Set and forget — check morning and evening.

techniques.name: Ojibwa Bird Snare
techniques.subtitle: Perch-triggered snare for birds

steps.instruction: Drive a pole into the ground near a bird-frequented area.
steps.helpDetail: Near water sources, berry bushes, or open fields where birds land. The pole should be about 4 feet tall.

steps.instruction: Drill a hole near the top for a perch toggle.
steps.helpDetail: The toggle is a short stick inserted through the hole. It must be loose enough to release when weight is applied.

steps.instruction: Attach a snare loop to the toggle, hanging down.
steps.helpDetail: The loop lies flat on top of the perch. When a bird lands, its weight pulls the toggle, tightening the snare around its feet.

steps.instruction: Attach a weight or spring to the snare line.
steps.helpDetail: A rock weight pulling the snare tight ensures the bird can't escape. The weight also swings the bird off the perch.

techniques.name: Fish Spear (Gig)
techniques.subtitle: Pronged spear for shallow water fishing

steps.instruction: Find a straight pole 6-8 feet long.
steps.helpDetail: Green hardwood is best — dry wood splits too easily. The pole should be about 1-inch diameter.

steps.instruction: Split the tip into 4 prongs, 6-8 inches deep.
steps.helpDetail: Cross-split the end. Insert small sticks to spread the prongs apart. Sharpen each prong to a point.

steps.instruction: Harden the tips in fire.
steps.helpDetail: Rotate the tips above coals (not in flames) until they darken. This case-hardens the wood. Don't burn them.

steps.instruction: Aim BELOW the fish — refraction makes fish appear higher than they are.
steps.helpDetail: Water bends light. The fish is actually 25% deeper than it looks. Aim at its shadow on the bottom if visible.

techniques.name: Fish Basket Trap
techniques.subtitle: Passive trap that catches fish 24/7

steps.instruction: Weave a funnel-shaped basket from flexible branches or vines.
steps.helpDetail: Willow, reed, or any pliable material. The funnel narrows inward — fish swim in but can't find the exit.

steps.instruction: Create an inner funnel that points inward.
steps.helpDetail: The opening should be 3-4 inches wide at the entrance, narrowing to 1-2 inches. Fish follow the funnel in but bump against the walls trying to get out.

steps.instruction: Bait with bread, berries, or fish guts inside the trap.
steps.helpDetail: Place bait at the far end. The smell draws fish in through the funnel.

steps.instruction: Set in streams facing upstream or in tidal areas.
steps.helpDetail: Fish swim upstream naturally and enter the funnel. Weight with rocks to keep in place. Check twice daily.

techniques.name: Improvised Hook & Line
techniques.subtitle: Fishing with found materials

steps.instruction: Make hooks from thorns, bones, safety pins, or carved wood.
steps.helpDetail: Gorge hooks (straight sharp sticks) work on large fish. Bend paperclips, wire, or use strong thorns. Bone fragments can be carved into hooks.

steps.instruction: Use any strong thin material for line.
steps.helpDetail: Paracord inner strands, dental floss, thread from clothing, plant fibers twisted into cordage. Test strength before using.

steps.instruction: Bait with insects, worms, berries, or bright material.
steps.helpDetail: Turn over rocks for insects. Dig for worms. Small pieces of bright cloth or feather can serve as lures.

steps.instruction: Set multiple passive lines to maximize chance.
steps.helpDetail: Tie lines to overhanging branches with bait in the water. Check periodically. This is more efficient than active fishing.

techniques.name: Edible Insects
techniques.subtitle: The most abundant protein source on Earth

steps.instruction: Target: ants, grasshoppers, crickets, grubs, termites.
steps.helpDetail: These are safe worldwide. Avoid brightly colored insects (warning coloration), hairy caterpillars, and anything that stinks.

steps.instruction: Always cook insects if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Roasting kills parasites. Dry-roast in a pan, on a hot rock, or skewer and hold over flame. Removes legs and wings for easier eating.

steps.instruction: Remove legs, wings, and heads before eating.
steps.helpDetail: Legs have barbs that irritate the throat. Wings are indigestible. Heads can be bitter.

steps.instruction: Collect in quantity — you need volume for meaningful nutrition.
steps.helpDetail: 100g of crickets has more protein than 100g of beef. But you need to eat a LOT. Set light traps at night or shake them from vegetation in the morning when cold.

techniques.name: Universal Edibility Test
techniques.subtitle: Test unknown plants before eating

steps.instruction: Test only ONE plant part at a time (leaf, root, fruit, stem).
steps.helpDetail: Each part may have different toxicity. Do not test more than one part simultaneously. Different parts of the same plant can range from edible to lethal.

steps.instruction: Rub on inner wrist for 15 minutes. Wait 45 minutes for reaction.
steps.helpDetail: If rash, burning, or swelling occurs — reject this plant. No reaction = proceed to next step.

steps.instruction: Touch to lip corner for 3 minutes. Then tongue tip for 15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Any tingling, burning, or numbness = stop. Spit out and rinse mouth. Wait 15 minutes between each step.

steps.instruction: Chew a small piece for 15 minutes without swallowing.
steps.helpDetail: If no burning, bitterness, or soapy taste, swallow. Wait 8 hours monitoring for stomach pain, nausea, or diarrhea.

steps.instruction: If no ill effects after 8 hours, eat 1/4 cup. Wait another 8 hours.
steps.helpDetail: If still no effects, the plant part is likely safe. This test takes 24+ hours. Do NOT shortcut. Poisoning in a survival situation can be fatal.

techniques.name: Commonly Edible Wild Plants
techniques.subtitle: Widely distributed safe plants

steps.instruction: Dandelion: entire plant is edible — leaves, flowers, roots.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves raw in salad, older leaves boiled to reduce bitterness. Roots roasted as coffee substitute. Grows worldwide.

steps.instruction: Cattail: the 'supermarket of the swamp.'
steps.helpDetail: Young shoots raw or cooked (taste like cucumber). Pollen as flour. Roots pounded for starch. Found at water edges worldwide.

steps.instruction: Pine: inner bark is edible, needles make vitamin C tea.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape the white/cream layer between bark and wood. Eat raw or dried and pounded into flour. Pine needle tea prevents scurvy.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pine inner bark and needle tea.

steps.instruction: Clover: flowers and leaves are edible raw or cooked.
steps.helpDetail: White and red clover are safe. High in protein for a plant. Dry and crumble for flour substitute.

steps.instruction: Plantain (broadleaf): common lawn weed, edible and medicinal.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves raw in salads. Older leaves cooked like spinach. Crushed leaf poultice for insect bites and stings.

techniques.name: Plants to Avoid
techniques.subtitle: Recognition of dangerous plants

steps.instruction: Avoid: white or yellow berries. 90% are poisonous.
steps.helpDetail: Exception: some white mulberries. But in a survival situation, skip ALL white/yellow berries. The risk is not worth it.

steps.instruction: Avoid: plants with milky sap (except dandelion and coconut).
steps.helpDetail: Milky sap often indicates toxic alkaloids. Even skin contact can cause blistering with some species.

steps.instruction: Avoid: umbrella-shaped flower clusters (parsley family).
steps.helpDetail: Water hemlock and poison hemlock are among the most lethal plants in North America. They look like carrots and parsley.

steps.instruction: Avoid: almond/peach scent in leaves or bark.
steps.helpDetail: This smell indicates cyanogenic glycosides — cyanide precursors. Cherry laurel, elderberry leaves, and wild almonds.

steps.instruction: Avoid: mushrooms unless you are an expert.
steps.helpDetail: Many deadly mushrooms look identical to edible ones. Amanita species kill 95% of people who eat them. No edibility test works for mushrooms. Experts only.

techniques.name: Cooking Over Open Fire
techniques.subtitle: Methods for cooking without pots

steps.instruction: Spit roasting: skewer meat on a green stick over coals.
steps.helpDetail: Use COALS, not flames. Flames char the outside while leaving the inside raw. Keep 6-8 inches above coals. Turn frequently.

steps.instruction: Rock cooking: heat flat rocks and cook directly on them.
steps.helpDetail: Use dry, non-porous rocks. WET or RIVER rocks can EXPLODE when heated — they contain trapped moisture. Heat gradually.

steps.instruction: Clay baking: coat meat in thick clay and bake in coals.
steps.helpDetail: The clay seals in moisture and creates an oven. When cracked open, skin/feathers peel off with the clay. Works great for birds and fish.

steps.instruction: Leaf wrapping: wrap food in large non-toxic leaves and place on coals.
steps.helpDetail: Banana leaves, burdock, grape leaves. Creates a steaming packet. Food cooks evenly and stays moist.

techniques.name: Stone Boiling
techniques.subtitle: Boil water in non-fireproof containers

steps.instruction: Fill a bark container, skin bag, or hollowed log with water.
steps.helpDetail: These containers can't go on fire but can hold boiling water. Even a plastic bag works if filled with water (the water prevents it from melting).

steps.instruction: Heat rocks in a fire until glowing hot.
steps.helpDetail: Use DRY rocks only. River rocks or wet rocks can shatter explosively. Heat for 20-30 minutes until glowing.

steps.instruction: Transfer hot rocks to the water with sticks or tongs.
steps.helpDetail: Brush ash off rocks before transferring. Drop carefully to avoid splashing boiling water.

steps.instruction: Continue adding rocks until water reaches a rolling boil.
steps.helpDetail: 2-3 fist-sized rocks will boil 1 liter. Remove cooled rocks with sticks and add newly heated ones to maintain the boil.

techniques.name: Meat Preservation (Smoking/Drying)
techniques.subtitle: Make meat last for weeks

steps.instruction: Cut meat into thin strips — 1/4 inch thick.
steps.helpDetail: Cut WITH the grain for jerky. Thinner strips dry faster and spoil less. Remove all fat — fat goes rancid.

steps.instruction: For smoking: build a low smoky fire in an enclosed space.
steps.helpDetail: Use green wood or wet wood to create dense smoke. Build a tripod or rack 2-3 feet above the fire. Smoke for 6-12 hours.

steps.instruction: For drying: hang strips in direct sun and wind.
steps.helpDetail: Hot, dry, windy conditions are ideal. Hang on a line or rack. Protect from flies with thin cloth. Sun-dry for 1-2 days.

steps.instruction: Meat is done when it cracks but doesn't break when bent.
steps.helpDetail: Properly dried meat can last 2-4 weeks without refrigeration. Store in breathable cloth or bark containers. Mold means it's too moist.

techniques.name: Pemmican
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie survival super food

steps.instruction: Pound dried meat into powder.
steps.helpDetail: Use rocks as mortar and pestle. Meat should be completely dry — any moisture causes spoilage. Pound until fibrous powder.

steps.instruction: Mix meat powder with rendered fat (50/50 ratio).
steps.helpDetail: Melt animal fat slowly. Strain out solids. Mix warm liquid fat with meat powder until it holds together.

steps.instruction: Add dried berries or nuts if available.
steps.helpDetail: Crushed dried berries add vitamins and flavor. Nuts add additional fat and calories. These are optional.

steps.instruction: Form into bars or balls and let cool.
steps.helpDetail: Pemmican stores for months at room temperature and years if kept cool. One pound provides 3,500 calories — a full day's rations.

techniques.name: Catching Crayfish
techniques.subtitle: Freshwater lobsters found in most streams

steps.instruction: Look under rocks in shallow streams and rivers.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish hide under rocks during the day. Grab behind the head to avoid pincers.

steps.instruction: Bait trap: container with small opening and bait inside.
steps.helpDetail: Cut plastic bottle with top inverted makes a trap. Bait with fish guts or bread.

steps.instruction: Boil 5-7 minutes until bright red. Eat tail meat.
steps.helpDetail: Never eat raw—parasites. Discard any dead before cooking.

techniques.name: Edible Seaweed Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: The ocean's most nutritious free food

steps.instruction: Harvest seaweed attached to rocks, not floating loose.
steps.helpDetail: Floating seaweed may be decomposing. Most attached seaweed is edible.

steps.instruction: Rinse in fresh water if available to reduce salt.
steps.helpDetail: High salt worsens dehydration. Eat in small amounts if no fresh water.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, dried, or add to soups. Rich in iodine and vitamins.
steps.helpDetail: Kelp, nori, dulse, sea lettuce all edible. Sun-dry for preservation.

techniques.name: Cattail Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Survival supermarket — every part is edible

steps.instruction: Identify: tall reeds with brown cigar-shaped seed heads.
steps.helpDetail: Found in marshes, ponds, wet areas worldwide. One of safest wild foods.

steps.instruction: Root: dig rhizome, peel and eat raw or roast. Starchy.
steps.helpDetail: Potato-like. Can be dried and ground into flour. Available year-round.

steps.instruction: Young shoots: peel outer leaves, inner core is edible raw.
steps.helpDetail: Called Cossack asparagus. Crisp, mild flavor. Spring harvest.

steps.instruction: Pollen: shake from flower head. High-protein flour substitute.
steps.helpDetail: Mix with water for pancakes. Summer harvest only.

techniques.name: Pine Needle Tea
techniques.subtitle: Vitamin C from any pine tree

steps.instruction: Gather green pine needles. Avoid yew (flat needles, red berries—toxic).
steps.helpDetail: True pines with needles in bundles of 2-5 are safe.

steps.instruction: Chop or bruise needles to release oils.
steps.helpDetail: Cut into small pieces or crush with rock.

steps.instruction: Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 10-15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling destroys vitamin C. Strain before drinking.

steps.instruction: 5x more vitamin C than orange juice. Prevents scurvy.
steps.helpDetail: Also contains vitamin A. Pleasant forest taste.

techniques.name: Smoking Meat for Preservation
techniques.subtitle: Preserve meat for weeks without refrigeration

steps.instruction: Cut meat into thin strips — 1/4 inch maximum.
steps.helpDetail: Thinner strips smoke faster and more evenly. Cut against grain.

steps.instruction: Build smoking rack 2-3 feet above small, smoky fire.
steps.helpDetail: Green wood tripod with cross-sticks. Smoke, not flame.

steps.instruction: Use hardwood: oak, hickory, maple, apple. Never pine.
steps.helpDetail: Resin from pine makes meat toxic. Green hardwood = most smoke.

steps.instruction: Smoke 6-12 hours until dry and dark. Should snap, not fold.
steps.helpDetail: Properly smoked jerky lasts 2-4 weeks unrefrigerated.

techniques.name: Animal Skinning & Butchering
techniques.subtitle: Field dress game safely

steps.instruction: Shallow cut from pelvis to ribcage — do not puncture organs.
steps.helpDetail: Use knife tip only. Lift skin, cut outward. Organ puncture contaminates meat.

steps.instruction: Remove organs in one mass. Save heart, liver, kidneys.
steps.helpDetail: Cut windpipe and esophagus at top. These organs are nutrient-dense.

steps.instruction: Skin by pulling hide away, cutting connective tissue.
steps.helpDetail: Start at legs, work toward spine. Keep blade against hide.

steps.instruction: Separate meat along muscle groups. Cook or preserve immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Hindquarters = most meat. Backstrap along spine = best cut.

techniques.name: Shellfish & Mussel Gathering
techniques.subtitle: Tidal zone protein available twice daily

steps.instruction: Gather at low tide. Mussels on rocks, clams in sand.
steps.helpDetail: Look for small holes that squirt water for clams.

steps.instruction: Discard any that are open and don't close when tapped.
steps.helpDetail: Open shells = dead = bacterial contamination.

steps.instruction: Boil until shells open, 5-10 minutes. Discard unopened.
steps.helpDetail: Raw shellfish carry parasites and bacteria.

steps.instruction: Avoid during red tide or warm months in tropics.
steps.helpDetail: Paralytic shellfish poisoning is fatal. No field treatment.

techniques.name: Wild Onion & Garlic ID
techniques.subtitle: Smells like onion = safe. No smell = death camas

steps.instruction: Crush leaf or bulb. MUST smell of onion or garlic.
steps.helpDetail: Death camas looks identical but has NO onion smell. Fatal if eaten.

steps.instruction: Check for onion-like bulb with papery skin.
steps.helpDetail: Layered bulbs like store-bought. Round with dry outer layers.

steps.instruction: Leaves should be grass-like, from the base.
steps.helpDetail: Long, flat or tubular. All from ground level.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, roasted, or in soups. Entire plant edible.
steps.helpDetail: Bulb, leaves, flowers all edible. Roasting sweetens.

techniques.name: Frog Catching
techniques.subtitle: Night hunting with a light source

steps.instruction: Hunt at night near water with light. Frogs freeze in light.
steps.helpDetail: Walk along stream banks and pond edges. Eyes reflect light.

steps.instruction: Grab quickly from behind. They are fast and slippery.
steps.helpDetail: Approach slowly, grab with fast swoop. Pin to ground.

steps.instruction: Avoid brightly colored frogs — many are toxic.
steps.helpDetail: Red, blue, yellow, orange = poison. Stick to green/brown.

steps.instruction: Remove skin, cook legs thoroughly. Discard organs.
steps.helpDetail: Skin may have irritants. Legs are the meat. Taste like chicken.

techniques.name: Pine Bark Flour
techniques.subtitle: Emergency starch from inner pine bark

steps.instruction: Strip the outer bark from a pine tree to expose the inner bark.
steps.helpDetail: The cambium layer — thin, moist, whitish layer between the rough outer bark and the wood. Peel in vertical strips. Take from multiple trees to avoid killing any one tree.

steps.instruction: Scrape off the inner bark (cambium) in thin strips.
steps.helpDetail: Use a knife edge to scrape. You want the soft, moist inner bark, not the hard outer bark or the wood underneath. It tastes slightly sweet and resinous.

steps.instruction: Dry the strips and grind into flour. Mix with water for porridge.
steps.helpDetail: Dried pine bark flour contains 500-600 calories per 100g. It can be eaten raw, boiled into porridge, or mixed with other flours. It kept Scandinavian populations alive through famine.

techniques.name: Cattail Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Every part of the cattail is edible year-round

steps.instruction: Identify cattails by their distinctive brown cigar-shaped seed head.
steps.helpDetail: Cattails grow in wetlands, ponds, and ditches worldwide. CAUTION: Iris leaves look similar but are toxic. Cattail leaves wrap around the stalk; iris leaves grow from the base in a fan.

steps.instruction: Spring: eat young shoots and stalk bases raw or cooked.
steps.helpDetail: The white base of young shoots tastes like cucumber. Pull the inner stalk out and eat the tender white portion. Called 'Cossack asparagus' by Russian settlers.

steps.instruction: Summer: harvest pollen from the male flower for protein-rich flour.
steps.helpDetail: Shake the yellow male flower (top spike) into a bag. The pollen is high in protein and can be mixed with regular flour for bread or added to soups.

steps.instruction: Fall/Winter: dig up the root rhizomes for starchy food.
steps.helpDetail: Wash and peel the roots. The starchy core can be eaten raw, boiled, or dried and ground to flour. Rhizomes are the highest calorie part of the plant.

techniques.name: Crayfish Trapping
techniques.subtitle: Easy protein from streams and ponds

steps.instruction: Look for crayfish in shallow, rocky streams and pond edges.
steps.helpDetail: Turn over rocks — crayfish hide underneath during the day. Active at night. Found on every continent except Antarctica.

steps.instruction: Build a simple funnel trap from sticks or a plastic bottle.
steps.helpDetail: Cut a bottle in half, invert the top into the bottom to create a funnel entrance. Or weave a cone from flexible sticks.

steps.instruction: Bait with fish scraps, meat, or strong-smelling food. Place in shallow water.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish are scavengers attracted to strong smells. Anchor the trap with rocks so it doesn't drift. Set in the evening for best results.

steps.instruction: Boil crayfish in water for 5 minutes. Eat the tail meat.
steps.helpDetail: MUST be cooked — crayfish carry parasites when raw. Twist the tail from the body, peel the shell, eat the meat.

techniques.name: Ojibwe Bird Snare
techniques.subtitle: Traditional bird snare using a bent sapling

steps.instruction: Find a flexible sapling near bird feeding areas.
steps.helpDetail: Look for areas where birds feed on the ground — berry bushes, seed heads, water edges.

steps.instruction: Bend the sapling down and secure it with a trigger mechanism.
steps.helpDetail: Carve a simple figure-4 trigger or use a toggle and notch system. The trigger holds the sapling down under tension.

steps.instruction: Attach a snare loop made from cordage to the trigger.
steps.helpDetail: The snare loop should be slightly larger than the bird's foot. Lay it flat around bait.

steps.instruction: Place bait (berries, seeds, insects) around the trigger.
steps.helpDetail: Set multiple snares in a line for better odds. Check every few hours.

techniques.name: Pemmican Making
techniques.subtitle: The ultimate survival food: meat, fat, and berries

steps.instruction: Dry lean meat into jerky, then pound into powder.
steps.helpDetail: Any lean meat works. Slice thin and dry completely over smoke or in the sun. Once bone-dry, pound between rocks until it's a fine, fluffy powder.

steps.instruction: Render fat by slowly heating it until it's liquid.
steps.helpDetail: Cut fat into small pieces, heat slowly. Strain the liquid fat through cloth. Pure rendered fat stores for months.

steps.instruction: Mix powdered meat with warm rendered fat at a 1:1 ratio.
steps.helpDetail: The fat should be warm but not hot. Mix thoroughly. Optionally add dried, crushed berries for nutrition and flavor.

steps.instruction: Press into bars. Stores for months without refrigeration.
steps.helpDetail: Pemmican is the most calorie-dense survival food: 3,000+ calories per pound. Keeps for years if stored dry.

techniques.name: Jerky Without Salt
techniques.subtitle: Preserve meat when you have no salt

steps.instruction: Cut meat into thin strips (1/4 inch) along the grain.
steps.helpDetail: Thinner strips dry faster. Remove ALL fat — fat does not dry, it goes rancid. Lean meat only.

steps.instruction: Build a drying rack over a smoky fire.
steps.helpDetail: Use green wood for more smoke (hickory, oak, mesquite). The smoke is antimicrobial and adds flavor.

steps.instruction: Hang strips on the rack 6-12 inches above the smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Don't let strips touch each other. Air must circulate. Flip every few hours.

steps.instruction: Dry until strips crack when bent but don't break.
steps.helpDetail: Takes 6-12 hours over smoke. Without salt, keeps 1-2 weeks. Eat it fast or make pemmican.

techniques.name: Mushroom Safety Rules
techniques.subtitle: Critical rules for mushroom identification and consumption

steps.instruction: NEVER eat a mushroom you cannot 100% identify. No exceptions.
steps.helpDetail: Many deadly mushrooms look almost identical to edible ones. Death cap (Amanita phalloides) kills more people than any other mushroom and initially looks like several edible species. There is NO universal test for edibility.

steps.instruction: Learn and focus ONLY on a few easily-identified safe species.
steps.helpDetail: Beginner-safe species with few lookalikes: chicken of the woods (orange shelf mushroom), giant puffball (must be pure white inside), morels (honeycomb pattern). Learn these from an expert, not a book alone.

steps.instruction: When in doubt, skip mushrooms. The caloric value isn't worth the risk.
steps.helpDetail: Most mushrooms are low in calories (20-30 cal per cup). A misidentification can cause liver failure and death. The risk/reward ratio is terrible. Focus caloric foraging on plants, insects, and animals instead.

techniques.name: Seaweed & Kelp Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Harvest nutritious seaweed from coastal environments

steps.instruction: Harvest seaweed from clean water, attached to rocks (not free-floating).
steps.helpDetail: Free-floating seaweed may be decomposing. Harvest from rocks in clean, moving water. Avoid areas near sewage outlets, harbors, or industrial sites. Best at low tide when more is exposed.

steps.instruction: Rinse thoroughly and dry in sun or near fire to preserve.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh seaweed spoils within 24 hours. Dried seaweed lasts months. Spread thin on rocks or hang on cord in sun. Can also eat fresh — most edible seaweed can be eaten raw or lightly boiled.

steps.instruction: Avoid blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) — it's toxic.
steps.helpDetail: Blue-green algae looks like green paint in water. It produces neurotoxins and liver toxins. Real seaweed is brown, green, or red and has a plant-like structure. If it looks like scum or slime, avoid it.

techniques.name: Pine Needle Tea
techniques.subtitle: Rich vitamin C source from any pine tree

steps.instruction: Gather a handful of fresh green pine needles. Avoid yew (toxic).
steps.helpDetail: Most pine species are safe: white pine, scotch pine, ponderosa. AVOID yew (flat needles, red berries) and Norfolk Island pine. Choose young, bright green needles for best flavor and nutrition.

steps.instruction: Chop needles into small pieces and steep in hot (not boiling) water.
steps.helpDetail: Chopping releases oils. Use hot water just below boiling — boiling destroys vitamin C. Steep 5-10 minutes until water turns light yellow-green. Longer steeping = stronger flavor.

steps.instruction: Drink as tea. Contains 5x the vitamin C of lemons by weight.
steps.helpDetail: Pine needle tea prevents scurvy and provides immune support. Pleasant, mild taste. Can be re-steeped 2-3 times. Also works cold-steeped overnight. The inner bark is also edible and calorie-dense.

techniques.name: Acorn Processing & Cooking
techniques.subtitle: Leach tannins from acorns to make edible flour

steps.instruction: Collect fallen acorns. Discard any with holes (insect larvae inside).
steps.helpDetail: White oak acorns are less bitter than red oak. All acorns are edible after processing. Crack shells and remove nutmeat. Brown, intact nutmeat is good. Black or moldy = discard.

steps.instruction: Crush nutmeat and soak in running water or change water repeatedly.
steps.helpDetail: Tannins make raw acorns bitter and cause stomach problems. Leaching methods: place crushed acorn in mesh bag in running stream for 24-48 hours, OR boil in multiple changes of water (5-7 changes until water runs clear).

steps.instruction: Dry leached acorn meal and grind into flour for cooking.
steps.helpDetail: Dried acorn flour can be used for flatbread, porridge, or thickening. 100g of acorn flour = 500+ calories. Acorns were a staple food for indigenous peoples worldwide. An oak tree produces 10,000-20,000 acorns per year.

techniques.name: Bird Snare Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build simple snares for catching birds

steps.instruction: Build an Ojibwa bird pole: tall pole with perch and snare.
steps.helpDetail: Drill a hole near top of a 6-foot pole. Insert a short perch stick. Tie a small noose of cordage around the perch. When a bird lands, its weight pulls the perch, tightening the noose around its feet.

steps.instruction: Place in open area near water or food sources where birds congregate.
steps.helpDetail: Birds prefer the highest perch available. Place your pole taller than surrounding vegetation. Near water is ideal — birds landing to drink will use your perch. Set multiple poles for better odds.

steps.instruction: Check snares frequently. Process caught birds immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Pluck feathers while still warm (easier). Gut immediately. Small birds can be roasted whole on a spit. All birds are edible — there are no poisonous birds (though some taste terrible). Cook thoroughly for food safety.

techniques.name: Small Game Field Dressing
techniques.subtitle: Clean and prepare small animals for cooking

steps.instruction: Make a small cut through the belly skin (avoid puncturing organs).
steps.helpDetail: Pinch belly skin and make a small cut. Insert two fingers under the skin to lift it away from organs, then cut between your fingers. Puncturing the intestines contaminates the meat.

steps.instruction: Remove intestines, stomach, and bladder. Keep liver, heart, and kidneys.
steps.helpDetail: Reach in and pull organs out in one mass. Heart, liver, and kidneys are the most nutritious parts — eat them first. Liver is extremely nutrient-dense. Check liver for spots (parasites) — discard if spotted.

steps.instruction: Skin by pulling hide off like removing a sock.
steps.helpDetail: For rabbits: cut around all four feet. Cut from foot to belly cut on each leg. Pull skin off like peeling a banana. Rinse carcass with clean water. Cook thoroughly — all wild game must be fully cooked.

techniques.name: Fish Spearing
techniques.subtitle: Improvise a spear and catch fish in shallow water

steps.instruction: Create a multi-pronged spear by splitting a sapling tip into 4 prongs.
steps.helpDetail: Choose a straight pole 6-8 feet long. Split the tip into 4 equal sections using your knife. Wedge a small stone between the prongs to spread them. Sharpen each prong. Bind below the split to prevent further splitting.

steps.instruction: Stand still in shallow water. Wait for fish to come to you.
steps.helpDetail: Patience is essential. Stand downstream and remain motionless. Fish will approach within minutes. Muddy your feet's scent by rubbing with mud before entering. Dawn and dusk are best times.

steps.instruction: Aim below the fish to compensate for water refraction.
steps.helpDetail: Light bends at the water surface, making the fish appear higher than it actually is. Aim at the bottom of the fish's shadow, not the fish itself. Thrust quickly and pin against the bottom.

techniques.name: Crayfish Trapping
techniques.subtitle: Easy, reliable protein from freshwater streams

steps.instruction: Build a simple funnel trap from a plastic bottle or woven sticks.
steps.helpDetail: Bottle trap: cut top off plastic bottle, invert it into the bottom half, staple/tie edges. Crayfish enter funnel but can't find the exit. Stick trap: weave a cone-shaped basket with narrow entrance.

steps.instruction: Bait with fish scraps, meat, or rotten food. Place in rocky stream areas.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish are scavengers attracted to strong-smelling bait. Place trap in 1-3 feet of water near rocks (crayfish habitat). Weight trap with rocks so it stays submerged. Set in evening, check in morning.

steps.instruction: Boil crayfish alive for 3-5 minutes until bright red. Eat tail meat.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT eat dead crayfish (toxin buildup). Twist tail from body, peel shell, eat tail meat. Claw meat is also edible on larger specimens. 100g of crayfish = 80 calories and 16g protein.

techniques.name: Finding & Eating Grubs
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie protein from rotting wood

steps.instruction: Look inside rotting logs and under bark for beetle larvae (grubs).
steps.helpDetail: Break apart soft, rotting wood. White or cream-colored larvae are beetle grubs. They're common in dead trees worldwide. Also check under rocks and in soil near rotting vegetation.

steps.instruction: Squeeze grubs gently to expel gut contents (optional but improves taste).
steps.helpDetail: Pinch behind the head and squeeze toward the tail to expel digestive contents. This removes bitter flavors. Not strictly necessary for safety — just preference.

steps.instruction: Roast on a stick near fire, or skewer and fry. Cook thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Roasted grubs taste nutty and buttery. A single large grub can contain 50+ calories — mostly fat and protein. 10 large grubs = a small meal. Can also be dried for storage.

techniques.name: Bone Broth for Maximum Nutrition
techniques.subtitle: Extract every calorie and mineral from animal bones

steps.instruction: Crack or smash bones to expose marrow before boiling.
steps.helpDetail: Marrow is the most calorie-dense part of an animal (80% fat). Use rocks to crack long bones. Marrow can also be roasted and eaten directly — push it out of the split bone with a stick.

steps.instruction: Boil cracked bones in water for 4-12 hours, keeping water level up.
steps.helpDetail: Long boiling extracts calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, and collagen from bones. The longer you boil, the more nutrition extracted. If possible, add acidic ingredients (wild berries) to extract more minerals.

steps.instruction: Drink broth hot. Skim fat from surface for cooking or calories.
steps.helpDetail: The fat floating on top is pure energy. Save it for cooking other foods. The broth provides minerals, electrolytes, and gelatin. Bones can be reboiled 2-3 times for diminishing but real returns.

techniques.name: Wild Garlic & Onion Identification
techniques.subtitle: Find wild alliums for nutrition and flavor

steps.instruction: Look for grass-like leaves with a strong garlic or onion smell when crushed.
steps.helpDetail: Wild garlic (ramps), wild onion, and wild leeks all have edible bulbs and leaves. The SMELL is the key identifier. If it doesn't smell like garlic or onion, DO NOT eat it — similar-looking plants (death camas) are deadly.

steps.instruction: Dig up the bulb. It should look like a small onion or garlic clove.
steps.helpDetail: The bulb should have layers (like an onion) or be solid (like garlic). If the bulb has no onion smell, it is NOT wild allium. Death camas has a similar leaf but no allium smell and its bulb has no layers.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, add to cooking, or dry for preservation.
steps.helpDetail: All parts are edible: bulbs, leaves, flowers. Excellent addition to bland survival food. Contains vitamin C, selenium, and has antimicrobial properties. Dry and crush leaves for seasoning that stores well.

techniques.name: Dandelion: Complete Edible Plant
techniques.subtitle: Every part of the dandelion is edible and nutritious

steps.instruction: Identify: jagged leaves, hollow stem with milky sap, yellow flower.
steps.helpDetail: Dandelions are found on every continent. Leaves form a rosette at ground level. Stem is hollow and produces white latex when broken. Flower is a single composite yellow head. No poisonous lookalikes.

steps.instruction: Harvest leaves (youngest are least bitter), flowers, and roots.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves: eat raw in salads or boil to reduce bitterness. Flowers: eat raw or fry (remove green parts — they're bitter). Roots: roast until dark and grind for coffee substitute, or boil as root vegetable.

steps.instruction: Rich in vitamins A, C, K, and minerals. More nutritious than spinach.
steps.helpDetail: Dandelion greens contain more vitamin A than carrots, more iron than spinach, and more calcium than milk per gram. Harvest away from roads (lead contamination) and lawns (herbicides).

techniques.name: Clay Pot Construction & Cooking
techniques.subtitle: Make a cooking vessel from natural clay

steps.instruction: Find clay near stream banks — it holds shape when squeezed and doesn't crumble.
steps.helpDetail: Good clay is smooth, sticky when wet, and holds its form. Test: roll a pencil-thin coil. If it bends without cracking, the clay is suitable. Remove stones and roots. Knead thoroughly to remove air bubbles.

steps.instruction: Build pot using coil method: roll long snakes of clay and stack in circles.
steps.helpDetail: Roll clay into ropes the thickness of your thumb. Coil upward from a flat base, smoothing each coil into the one below. Keep walls even thickness (1/2 inch). Smooth inside and out. Make walls thicker at the base.

steps.instruction: Dry slowly for 24-48 hours, then fire in a hot campfire for 2-3 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Fast drying causes cracking. Place near fire for gentle drying first. Then bury in hot coals for 2-3 hours. Let cool slowly in the ashes. The pot will be fragile but functional for boiling water and cooking stews.

techniques.name: Earth Oven (Ground Pit Cooking)
techniques.subtitle: Cook large quantities of food using heated stones underground

steps.instruction: Dig a pit 2-3 feet deep and wide. Build a large fire in it with stones.
steps.helpDetail: Use dense, non-porous stones (river rocks can explode from trapped water). Burn a hot fire for 2-3 hours until stones are glowing. Scrape out coals but leave hot stones in the pit.

steps.instruction: Layer: hot stones, wet leaves/grass, food, wet leaves/grass, more hot stones.
steps.helpDetail: The wet vegetation creates steam for cooking. Wrap food in large green leaves to protect from ash. Layer food that takes longest on the bottom (root vegetables) and fastest on top (greens, fish).

steps.instruction: Cover with earth and let cook 4-8 hours. The earth insulates the heat.
steps.helpDetail: Seal edges with packed dirt so steam can't escape. This method slow-cooks everything evenly. Root vegetables become soft and sweet. Meat falls off the bone. Can cook for a large group efficiently.

techniques.name: Emergency Rationing Strategy
techniques.subtitle: How to eat when food is scarce

steps.instruction: Inventory EVERYTHING edible immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Count every bar, cracker, and candy. Divide total calories by days until rescue (estimated). Goal: 500+ cal/day.

steps.instruction: Do NOT eat for the first 24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Your body has reserves. Eating triggers digestion which uses water. Save food for when hunger pangs stabilize.

steps.instruction: Eat small amounts during activity peaks.
steps.helpDetail: Eat before heavy exertion (hiking/building) or before sleep (to stay warm). Don't graze all day.

techniques.name: Wild Fruit Identification
techniques.subtitle: Identify safe edible wild fruits

steps.instruction: Use the Universal Edibility Test: test ONE plant part at a time.
steps.helpDetail: Never eat unknown plants without testing. Test only one plant and one part (fruit, leaf, stem) at a time. If you react to any part, discard the entire plant.

steps.instruction: Avoid: white/yellow berries, umbrella-shaped flowers, milky sap.
steps.helpDetail: General rule: 90% of white and yellow berries are poisonous. Plants with milky sap or umbrella flower clusters (like poison hemlock) are dangerous. Red berries are 50/50.

steps.instruction: Safe bets: blackberries, raspberries, blueberries, rose hips.
steps.helpDetail: These are widely distributed and easy to identify. Blackberries/raspberries have compound berries on bramble bushes. Rose hips are the red fruit below rose flowers.

steps.instruction: Smell test: crush a small amount and smell. Reject if bitter or almond-like.
steps.helpDetail: Bitter almond smell indicates cyanide compounds. Soapy smell indicates saponins. Both are toxic. A pleasant, fruity smell is a good sign but not conclusive.

steps.instruction: Skin test: rub on inner wrist, wait 15 minutes for reaction.
steps.helpDetail: If no rash, burning, or numbness, place a small amount on your lip. Wait 15 minutes. Then on tongue. Wait 15 minutes. Then chew and spit. If no reaction after 8 hours, eat a small amount.

articles.title: Caloric Needs in Survival
articles.body:
Your body needs fuel. Understanding caloric needs helps prioritize food sources.

Daily caloric needs:
- Resting in shelter: 1,200-1,500 cal
- Light activity: 2,000-2,500 cal
- Heavy activity (building, hiking): 3,000-4,000 cal
- Cold weather: add 500-1,000 cal for thermogenesis

Caloric density of survival foods:
- Fat: 9 cal/gram (most efficient)
- Protein: 4 cal/gram
- Carbohydrates: 4 cal/gram
- Insects: 400-500 cal per 100g
- Pine inner bark: 500-600 cal per 100g
- Pemmican: 250 cal per ounce

The body can survive 3 weeks without food but performance degrades rapidly after 3 days. Cognitive function and decision-making decline first — exactly when you need them most.

articles.title: Trapping vs. Hunting: Efficiency
articles.body:
Trapping is dramatically more efficient than hunting in survival situations.

Hunting:
- Requires weapon, skill, and energy expenditure
- You pursue ONE animal at a time
- Success rate for untrained hunters: ~5%
- Burns 300-600 calories per hour

Trapping:
- Set once, works 24/7
- Multiple traps = multiple chances simultaneously
- Set 12 snares for ~1 hr effort, catching odds improve to 50%+
- Burns 0 additional calories while waiting

Best practice: Set 10-12 snares on active game trails. Refresh every 48 hours. Check twice daily. The energy investment in setting traps pays dividends.

Ethical note: Check traps regularly. An animal suffering in a trap attracts predators to your area and wastes meat to stress hormones.

articles.title: Insects as Survival Food
articles.body:
Insects are the world's most underutilized food source. Over 2 billion people eat insects daily.

Nutrition per 100g:
- Crickets: 21g protein, 6g fat, 121 calories
- Grasshoppers: 20g protein, 6g fat, 153 calories
- Mealworms: 20g protein, 13g fat, 206 calories
- Termites: 14g protein, 2g fat, 535 calories
- Ants: 14g protein, 4g fat, 100 calories

Safe to eat (worldwide):
- Ants (not fire ants — remove heads), crickets, grasshoppers, beetle larvae/grubs, termites, cicadas, scorpions (remove stinger, cook)

Avoid:
- Brightly colored insects (warning coloration = toxic)
- Hairy caterpillars (urticating hairs cause allergic reactions)
- Anything that stinks when crushed
- Ticks, mosquitoes, flies (disease vectors)
- Spiders (not insects but commonly confused)

Collection methods: Turn over logs and rocks, shake vegetation in early morning when cold, attract with light at night.

articles.title: Fishing in Survival Situations
articles.body:
Fish are the easiest wild protein to catch with improvised gear.

Best times: Dawn and dusk. Fish feed most actively in low light.

Best locations:
- Where streams meet (confluences)
- Behind rocks in flowing water (fish rest in eddies)
- Undercut banks and overhanging vegetation
- Deeper pools in shallow streams
- Inlets and outlets of lakes

Passive fishing > active fishing. Set trotlines (multiple baited hooks on a line anchored at both ends) and fish traps, then go do other survival tasks.

Legal note: In survival situations, fish and game laws still technically apply. However, courts have consistently recognized 'necessity defense' for genuine survival situations.

Preservation: Fish spoils fastest of all meats. Cook immediately or smoke/dry within 2 hours of catching.

articles.title: Poisonous Plants: Warning Signs
articles.body:
When in doubt, do NOT eat it. These signs suggest a plant may be toxic:

- Milky or discolored sap (exception: dandelion)
- Beans, bulbs, or seeds inside pods
- Bitter or soapy taste
- Spines, thorns, or fine hairs
- 'Almond' scent in leaves or wood (= cyanide compounds)
- Three-leaflet growth pattern (poison ivy/oak)
- Umbrella-shaped flower clusters (hemlock family)
- White or yellow berries (90% toxic)
- Grain heads with pink, purple, or black spurs (ergot fungus)

Specific plants to memorize as deadly:
- Water hemlock (most violent plant toxin in North America)
- Death camas (looks like wild onion)
- Amanita mushrooms (death cap, destroying angel)
- Castor bean (contains ricin)
- Oleander (every part is lethal)
- Nightshade (berries look edible)

If plant poisoning occurs: induce vomiting if conscious and within 1 hour of ingestion. Drink large amounts of water. Seek medical care.

articles.title: Food Preservation Without Refrigeration
articles.body:
In a survival situation, you cannot afford to waste food. Preservation methods:

Smoking (6-12 hours):
- Creates acidic surface that inhibits bacteria
- Adds antimicrobial compounds from wood smoke
- Result lasts 1-4 weeks

Drying/Dehydrating (1-3 days):
- Removes moisture bacteria need to grow
- Requires hot, dry, windy conditions
- Result lasts 2-4 weeks

Salting:
- Pack in salt or create a brine
- Draws moisture out through osmosis
- Result lasts months

Cold storage:
- Bury in stream beds (cold running water)
- Hang in shade at altitude (cool air)
- Cache in snow or permafrost

Fat rendering:
- Slowly heat fat until liquid
- Strain and store — pure rendered fat lasts months
- Used in pemmican production

Critical rule: when in doubt, smell it. Spoiled meat has a distinct sweet-putrid smell that's unmistakable. Trust your nose — it evolved specifically for this.

articles.title: Nutritional Priorities in Survival
articles.body:
In survival, calories matter more than balanced nutrition short-term.

Priority hierarchy:
1. Calories (energy to function)
2. Water (can't digest food without hydration)
3. Fat (most calorie-dense — 9 cal/gram)
4. Protein (muscle maintenance, immune function)
5. Carbohydrates (quick energy, brain fuel)
6. Micronutrients (Vitamin C prevents scurvy after 30 days)

Starvation timeline:
- Day 1-3: Glycogen depletion
- Day 3-7: Cognitive decline, irritability
- Day 7-14: Muscle breakdown for energy
- Day 14-21: Severe weakness, organ stress
- Day 21+: Cardiac irregularity risk

Performance declines severely after 72 hours. Even small amounts of food (insects, bark, roots) maintain cognitive function.

articles.title: Wild Plant Basics
articles.body:
Plants are the most accessible food source but also dangerous if misidentified.

Always-safe plants worldwide:
- Dandelion (entire plant edible)
- Cattail (shoots, roots, pollen year-round)
- Pine (inner bark, needles for tea, nuts)
- Clover (flowers and leaves)
- Plantain weed (leaves edible and medicinal)

Universal Edibility Test:
1. Test on skin (wait 15 min)
2. Touch to lip (wait 15 min)
3. Touch to tongue (wait 15 min)
4. Chew and spit (wait 15 min)
5. Eat small amount (wait 8 hours)

WARNING: This test does NOT work for mushrooms — some deadly mushrooms taste pleasant and show no symptoms for 6-12 hours before organ failure.

Cooking makes many marginal plants safer by breaking down irritants and toxins. When in doubt, boil it.

articles.title: Food Safety Without Refrigeration
articles.body:
Food poisoning in survival can be fatal — dehydration from vomiting depletes limited water reserves.

General rules:
- Cook ALL meat thoroughly (165°F/74°C)
- Eat food within 2 hours of cooking if not preserved
- Smell test: if it smells off, don't eat it
- Never eat meat from animals found already dead
- Avoid scavengers' kills (possible disease)

High-risk foods to avoid:
- Raw freshwater fish (parasites)
- Raw shellfish from warm waters
- Any plant with milky sap (except dandelion)
- Unidentified mushrooms
- Grain with black/purple growths (ergot fungus)

Safe handling:
- Clean hands before prep (ash works as soap)
- Separate raw meat from other foods
- Cook on clean surfaces
- Store surplus elevated and sealed

The survival paradox: you need food for energy, but contaminated food kills faster than starvation.

articles.title: Starvation Physiology: What Happens to Your Body
articles.body:
Understanding starvation helps you prioritize and conserve energy.

Phase 1 (0-24 hours): Glycogen depletion
- Body burns stored glycogen (sugar) in liver and muscles
- Blood sugar remains normal
- Performance is near normal
- Strong hunger signals

Phase 2 (1-3 days): Fat burning begins
- Body switches to burning fat for energy (ketosis)
- Hunger may actually decrease (ketones suppress appetite)
- Mental fog and irritability
- Fatigue during physical activity

Phase 3 (3-14 days): Sustained fat burning
- Body adapts to fat-burning metabolism
- Weight loss of 1-2 pounds per day
- Muscle wasting begins as body needs protein for vital functions
- Cold intolerance increases (less insulation, less metabolic heat)

Phase 4 (14+ days): Critical decline
- Muscle wasting accelerates
- Immune function collapses (infections become deadly)
- Mental function deteriorates severely
- Organ failure begins

Key takeaway: the body can survive 3+ weeks without food, but cognitive and physical performance degrades within days. In a survival situation, your brain failing to make good decisions is more dangerous than the starvation itself.

articles.title: Plant Poisoning: Recognition & Treatment
articles.body:
Misidentification of wild plants is the leading cause of foraging deaths.

Dangerous plant families:
- Hemlock (Conium, Cicuta): looks like carrot/parsley. Causes respiratory paralysis. Fatal.
- Death camas: looks like wild onion but NO onion smell. Causes heart failure.
- Nightshade: black or red berries. Neurological symptoms. Can be fatal.
- Castor bean: seeds contain ricin. A few chewed seeds can kill.
- Oleander: all parts toxic. Even smoke from burning is dangerous.

Universal warning signs in plants (avoid if present):
- Milky sap (exception: dandelion)
- Shiny leaves with thorns
- Seeds inside pods
- Bitter or soapy taste
- Umbrella-shaped flower clusters (many toxic species)

Poisoning treatment:
- If conscious: do NOT induce vomiting (may cause more damage)
- Activated charcoal if available (1g per kg body weight)
- Drink large amounts of water to dilute
- If convulsing: protect the head, maintain airway
- Red flag: any difficulty breathing, hallucinations, or loss of consciousness = critical emergency

Prevention rule: NEVER eat any plant you cannot identify with 100% certainty. The universal edibility test takes 24+ hours and should only be used as a last resort.

articles.title: Survival Protein Sources: Ranked by Effort
articles.body:
Not all protein sources are equal. Rank by calories gained versus calories expended to acquire them.

Best return on effort (passive methods):
1. Insects & grubs: everywhere, easy to collect, high calorie. Best ROI in survival.
2. Crayfish/shellfish: trap overnight, minimal effort, reliable.
3. Snares/traps: set and walk away. Check twice daily. 10+ traps = good odds.
4. Fishing (passive): set lines, trotlines, fish traps. Work while you sleep.

Moderate effort:
5. Fishing (active): hook and line, spearing. Hours of investment but reliable in good water.
6. Frogs: easy to catch by hand at night with a light source.
7. Birds (snares): passive but lower success rate than mammal snares.

High effort (often not worth it in short-term survival):
8. Large game hunting: requires weapon, skill, tracking. Very high energy expenditure.
9. Running down small game: exhausting and rarely successful.

Critical insight: a person who sets 12 snares and spends the day collecting insects and fishing with passive lines will outperform a person who spends all day stalking deer. The deer hunter exerts 3,000+ calories and may get nothing. The passive forager expends 500 calories and reliably gathers 1,000+.

Rule: work smarter, not harder. Passive calorie acquisition beats active hunting in survival.

articles.title: Long-Term Food Preservation Methods
articles.body:
Surplus food spoils quickly. Preservation extends your food supply for days or weeks.

Drying/Dehydration:
- Most universal method. Works on meat, fish, plants, fruits, insects.
- Cut thin strips (1/4 inch). Hang in sun and wind, or smoke near fire.
- Properly dried food lasts weeks to months.
- Test: should be brittle, not bendy. Bendy = still moist = will rot.

Smoking:
- Smoke contains antimicrobial compounds that preserve meat.
- Cold smoking (below 90°F): best for preservation — 12-24 hours minimum.
- Hot smoking (above 150°F): cooks and partially preserves — eat within a week.
- Build a simple smokehouse: tripod frame, covered with bark or canvas.

Salt curing:
- Salt dehydrates bacteria. Pack meat in salt (if available).
- Salt + drying = most effective combination.
- Ocean water: boil to extract salt crystals.

Pemmican (ultimate survival food):
- Dried meat powder + rendered fat + dried berries.
- Lasts months to years if properly made.
- Calorie-dense: 135 cal per ounce.
- Complete nutrition: protein, fat, carbohydrates, vitamins.

Freezing (cold climates):
- Simply hang food above predator reach in freezing temperatures.
- Thaw and refreeze degrades quality — keep frozen solid.

articles.title: Primitive Cooking Methods
articles.body:
Cooking improves digestibility, kills pathogens, and makes many foods edible that are toxic raw.

Direct heat:
- Spit roasting: skewer food on stick over fire. Rotate constantly. Best for small game and fish.
- Planking: pin food to flat wood propped near fire. Great for fish fillets.
- Ember roasting: wrap food in green leaves, bury in hot coals. Root vegetables and tubers.

Boiling without a metal pot:
- Rock boiling: heat stones in fire, drop into water in bark/skin/stomach container. Water boils in minutes.
- Bamboo cooking: fill bamboo segment with water and food, prop over fire. Bamboo chars but doesn't burn through before food cooks.
- Clay pot: make a simple pot from clay (see technique).

Earth oven:
- Pit filled with hot rocks, food layered between wet leaves.
- Slow cooks large quantities — feeds groups.
- 4-8 hours cooking time.

Why cooking matters:
- Doubles the caloric yield from meat (cooked protein is 60% digestible vs 30% raw)
- Kills parasites (trichinella, tapeworm, toxoplasma)
- Breaks down plant toxins (tannins, oxalates, cyanogenic glycosides)
- Makes starchy roots digestible (raw starch passes through undigested)

Rule: always cook when possible. Raw food is a caloric and health gamble.

articles.title: Nutritional Deficiencies in Survival
articles.body:
Long-term survival requires more than just calories. Specific deficiencies create specific problems.

Vitamin C deficiency (Scurvy):
- Onset: 4-12 weeks without vitamin C
- Symptoms: bleeding gums, loose teeth, wounds won't heal, fatigue
- Sources: pine needle tea, rose hips, spruce tips, dandelion greens, berries
- Fix: any fresh plant material provides vitamin C

Salt deficiency (Hyponatremia):
- Risk: heavy sweating + drinking lots of water without salt
- Symptoms: nausea, headache, confusion, seizures
- Sources: sea water (boil to concentrate), animal blood, wood ash (contains potassium)

Fat deficiency (Rabbit starvation):
- Risk: eating only lean protein (rabbit, squirrel) without fat
- Symptoms: diarrhea, headache, fatigue, low blood pressure
- Your body CANNOT survive on protein alone — fat is essential
- Sources: bone marrow, brain tissue, grubs, nuts, fish oils

Iron deficiency:
- Risk: vegetarian survival diet, chronic blood loss
- Symptoms: weakness, rapid heartbeat, cold intolerance
- Sources: organ meat (liver), dark leafy greens, insects, cooking in iron vessels

Key insight: a varied diet solves most deficiency problems. Eat from every food group available: plants, meat, insects, fish. Organ meats are the most nutritionally complete single food source.

steps.instruction: Verify ID with 3 distinct features.
steps.helpDetail: Leaf shape, berry color, stem type. Do NOT eat if any feature doesn't match your memory/guide exactly.

steps.instruction: Smell: should smell like fruit, not almond or rotting.
steps.helpDetail: Bitter almond smell can indicate cyanide.

steps.instruction: Eat small amount first.
steps.helpDetail: Wait 30 minutes to check for reaction before eating a meal.

steps.instruction: Avoid: White or Red berries unless known.
steps.helpDetail: 'White and Red are dead, Blue and Black usually snack' (but not always!). 90% of white berries are toxic.
